shock_exposure: 4.785 mb/d 2024 Hormuz crude destination volume; CGEP summarizes broader China Hormuz crude transit exposure at 45-50% of crude imports.
evidence_type: observed exposure plus observed refinery-run cuts; replacement/inventory split inferred
caveats: Low/base/high are near-term daily flow-equivalent judgment ranges, not observed cargo accounting. Public data support crude import and run cuts but do not separate government SPR, commercial inventory, bonded barrels, sanctioned barrels, or replacement-origin volumes.

shock_exposure: 1.885 mb/d 2024 Hormuz crude destination volume in importer matrix; Government of India said about 45% of pre-crisis crude imports used Hormuz and non-Hormuz sourcing rose from about 55% to about 70%.
evidence_type: official substitution signal plus repo exposure matrix; inventory/demand split inferred
caveats: Replacement estimate is anchored to the reported 15 percentage-point non-Hormuz sourcing shift, scaled conservatively to the EIA/Vortexa destination exposure row. India crude demand destruction evidence is weak compared with gas/LPG rationing evidence.

shock_exposure: 1.515 mb/d 2024 Hormuz crude destination volume; METI says Middle East normally supplies more than 90% of Japan crude imports.
evidence_type: official replacement-procurement and stock-release statements plus repo exposure matrix
caveats: Replacement range maps METI's 60% May and 70%+ June alternative-procurement statements to the 2024 route-exposure row. Inventory draw is a bridge estimate from public/private reserve-release policy, not a measured drawdown series.

shock_exposure: 1.727 mb/d 2024 Hormuz crude destination volume; high Middle East crude reliance and Qatar condensate/LNG exposure noted in F6R.3.
evidence_type: repo exposure matrix plus diplomatic/supplier-assurance evidence; quantitative split mostly inferred
caveats: Korea has clear exposure and reserve capacity evidence but less public evidence on actual replacement crude volumes or demand curtailment. Treat as a preliminary bridge row until customs, refinery-run, or stock-draw data are reconciled.

shock_exposure: 0.721 mb/d 2024 Hormuz crude destination volume to Europe, plus product/jet-fuel price and localized availability exposure.
evidence_type: repo exposure matrix plus Europe exposure classification; demand response partly inferred from Commission fuel-saving guidance
caveats: EU aggregate direct crude exposure is moderate and replacement optionality is stronger than in Northeast Asia; Mediterranean refinery/product stress can be higher than the aggregate. Product row overlaps with crude and should not be added mechanically.

shock_exposure: 0.484 mb/d 2024 Hormuz crude destination volume; EIA describes this as about 7% of U.S. crude/condensate imports and 2% of U.S. petroleum liquids consumption.
evidence_type: EIA exposure anchor plus S49 reserve-response table; global price exposure larger than direct volume risk
caveats: The U.S. can physically replace direct Gulf barrels, but consumers and firms still face global crude/product price pass-through. Inventory draw mixes SPR exchange availability and commercial stock movement; not all announced reserves become net supply.

shock_exposure: About 99 bcm/year of Qatar/UAE Hormuz LNG exposure in importer matrix; IEA says almost 90% of Hormuz LNG exports go to Asia and more than one-quarter of Asia LNG imports transit Hormuz.
evidence_type: IEA/EIA exposure anchor plus S49 LNG buffer table; adjustment split inferred
caveats: No practical seaborne bypass exists for Qatar/UAE LNG, so replacement is constrained by spare spot LNG, receiving-terminal capacity, contracts, and price willingness. Demand destruction includes fuel switching and industrial/power curtailment; country allocation remains weak.

shock_exposure: CGEP summarizes 31% of China's 2025 LNG imports from the Middle East, led by Qatar at 28%; public daily storage inventory is unavailable.
evidence_type: observed exposure; storage and demand response inferred from F6R.2/S49.4
caveats: Use qualitative bands because China-specific replacement LNG volumes and gas inventory draws are not publicly separable. Best public inference is less spot LNG buying, fuel switching, and industrial/power demand response rather than clean supplier replacement.

shock_exposure: Qatar supplied about 4% of Japan LNG imports; METI reported roughly three weeks of LNG stock and IEA gives about 36 days of capacity ceiling.
evidence_type: official stock/exposure statements plus S49 LNG buffer table
caveats: Japan's LNG exposure is much smaller than its crude exposure. Demand destruction is likely limited unless the shock persists beyond tank and procurement buffers; public LNG stock reporting is not a regular daily series.

shock_exposure: Qatar is South Korea's third-largest LNG supplier; KOGAS reserve rule equals 7 days mandatory plus 30 days preventive reserve, but contemporaneous fill is not public.
evidence_type: official supplier-assurance evidence plus S49 LNG buffer table; demand response inferred
caveats: The public record supports high exposure and a formal inventory rule, but not a measured drawdown or replacement-cargo schedule. Condensate overlaps with crude/petrochemical channels.

shock_exposure: Government releases describe 47.4 MMSCMD of gas affected by force majeure and a 30 MMSCMD Gulf-source gas disruption; official orders prioritized households/CNG and cut industry/fertilizer/refinery/petrochemical users.
evidence_type: official disruption and allocation-order evidence; replacement/inventory split inferred
caveats: This is one of the strongest demand-destruction rows because rationing percentages are public. Inventory draw is low because no national public gas-storage inventory comparable to GIE/KOGAS was found.

shock_exposure: IEA/F6R.1 row records around two-thirds of India's LPG consumption transiting Hormuz and about 0.43 mb/d of imports disrupted early in the conflict; India says about 60% of LPG consumption is imported and roughly 90% of imports come through Hormuz.
evidence_type: official LPG control measures plus IEA/repo exposure anchor; split inferred
caveats: Replacement supply includes extra domestic LPG yield and alternative suppliers; demand destruction captures commercial allocation limits and booking-gap changes. Rows do not sum cleanly because some refinery C3/C4 diversion moves scarcity into petrochemicals.

product_or_channel: crude products LNG LPG and petrochemical feedstocks
shock_exposure: IEA says pre-crisis Southeast Asia sourced around 60% of crude imports and one-third of gas imports from the Middle East, with 45% of oil product supply dependent on Middle Eastern crude.
evidence_type: IEA regional exposure and policy-response evidence; country-level volumes not reconciled
caveats: Keep as a regional band until Singapore, Thailand, Viet Nam, Philippines, Indonesia, and Malaysia are split. Demand destruction is more visible than inventory draw through public transport, remote-work, subsidies/price controls, and fuel switching evidence.

shock_exposure: F6R.1/KMZ/S49 rows place upstream Hormuz countries at 34% of global urea trade; impacts largest in South Asia and Latin America, with India stock proxies available but no global daily stock ledger.
evidence_type: IFASTAT/World Bank/repo fertilizer buffers; importer split and inventories partly inferred
caveats: Use as fertilizer availability/cost pressure, not a direct food-output loss estimate. Demand destruction can mean delayed purchases, lower application, or subsidy rationing; timing depends on planting season and domestic policy.

shock_exposure: F6R.1 records 3.8 Mt/year Gulf sulfur exposure for China and 3.7 Mt/year for Morocco/OCP; IEA/IFA rows say roughly half of seaborne sulfur trade moves through Hormuz.
evidence_type: repo importer exposure plus IEA/IFA global exposure; replacement and demand response inferred
caveats: Sulfur has strong global Hormuz exposure but weak public stock data. Demand destruction here means lower phosphate/sulfuric-acid operating rates or deferred production, not final consumer demand cuts.

shock_exposure: IEA/KMZ master table reports about 5 Mt/year of Gulf aluminium shipped through Hormuz and Gulf producers around 8% of global supply.
evidence_type: IEA/KMZ exposure anchor; importer split not public in current matrix
caveats: Replacement is constrained by primary smelter capacity, inventories, scrap availability, and long supply chains. Current public repo inputs do not map Gulf aluminium to destination-country demand.
